sentences.grammarNote:
**soy** — first person singular of **ser**. Used for name/identity — a permanent trait.
Bad Bunny's real name is Benito Antonio Martínez Ocasio. Stage names are common in reggaetón.

sentences.base_language: I am a very famous music artist
sentences.target_language: Soy un artista de música muy famoso
sentences.grammarNote:
**ser** for profession and identity: **soy artista** (I'm an artist).
**artista** ends in -a but can be masculine or feminine: **el artista** (male) · **la artista** (female).
**famoso** agrees masculine with **artista** (masculine here).

sentences.base_language: I sang at the super bowl
sentences.grammarNote:
**canté** — preterite of **cantar** (regular -ar verb): I sang. The preterite expresses a completed past action.
**en el super bowl** — **en** for events/locations. Note the accent on **canté** (required in preterite yo for -ar verbs).

**Soy de** = I'm from — origin always uses **ser**.
Puerto Rico is a U.S. territory with Spanish as an official language — Bad Bunny is a major ambassador of Puerto Rican culture and reggaetón music.

sentences.base_language: Do you want to learn one of my songs?
sentences.grammarNote:
**quieres** — **querer** stem-changes e→ie.
**una de mis canciones** = one of my songs — **de mis** (of my): possessive **mis** (plural of **mi**).
**canciones** = songs — singular: **canción** (ends in -ón, plural drops accent: **canciones**).

**Esta** (feminine) refers to **la canción** (the song) — feminine noun.
**preguntó** — preterite of **preguntar** (to ask): she asked. The accent on **-ó** marks third person singular preterite.
The song title is in Puerto Rican slang: **tití** = auntie (diminutive-affectionate form).

sentences.base_language: Aunti asked me if I have so many girlfriends
sentences.target_language: Tití me preguntó si tengo muchas novias
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tití** = auntie in Caribbean Spanish — the **-í** ending is an informal affectionate form.
**me preguntó si** = asked me if/whether — indirect question uses **si** (if/whether, no accent).
**muchas novias** — **muchas** (fem. pl.) agrees with **novias** (fem. pl.).

sentences.base_language: Today I have one, tomorrow I'll have another, eh
sentences.target_language: Hoy tengo una, mañana otra, eh
sentences.grammarNote:
**mañana otra** — the future verb is omitted entirely (understood from context: *tendré otra*).
**una** and **otra** are feminine, referring to **novia** (girlfriend) — gender agreement even when the noun is implicit.
**eh** = a filler/emphasis particle — very common in Caribbean Spanish.

sentences.base_language: hey, but there's no wedding, there's no wedding, auntie
sentences.target_language: ey, pero no boda, no boda, tití
sentences.grammarNote:
**no boda** — the verb **hay** is omitted for rhythmic effect in the lyrics: *no hay boda* (there's no wedding).
**boda** = wedding (feminine) — from Latin *vota* (vows).
Repetition for emphasis is a common device in reggaetón lyrics.

sentences.base_language: I'm going to take them all to the V.I.P., the V.I.P., hey
sentences.target_language: Me las voy a llevar a todas pa un V.I.P., un V.I.P.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me las voy a llevar** is a complex construction: **me** is an indirect object (for myself), **las** refers to **ellas** (them), and **voy a llevar** is the near future (**ir + a + infinitive**).
**pa** = **para** (Puerto Rican/Caribbean colloquial shortening) — very common in informal speech and song lyrics.
**a todas** = all of them (feminine) — **todas** referring to the women.

**Dile** = tell her/say to her — imperative of **decir** + indirect object **le** (to her).
**a la tití** — the personal **a** before a specific person as indirect object.
**Dile** is extremely common: **dile que venga** (tell her to come) · **dile hola** (say hi to her).

sentences.base_language: Let's take a selfie, "say cheese," hey
sentences.target_language: Vamo a tirarno un selfi, "say cheese," ey
sentences.grammarNote:
**Vamo a tirarno** = colloquial Caribbean Spanish for **vamos a tomarnos** (let's take [a photo of] ourselves).
**tirar** in Caribbean Spanish can mean to take (a photo), throw, or shoot — context-dependent slang.
**selfi** = selfie — adapted loanword. Spanish often adapts English loanwords to Spanish spelling.

sentences.base_language: Let the ones I already slept with smile
sentences.target_language: Que sonrían las que ya conmigo durmieron
sentences.grammarNote:
**Que sonrían** uses the subjunctive to express a wish or command directed at a third party: 'let them smile'.
**durmieron** — preterite of **dormir** (o→u stem change in preterite): **dormí · dormiste · durmió · dormimos · durmieron**.
**conmigo** = with me — fused preposition.

sentences.base_language: In a V.I.P., a V.I.P., hey
sentences.target_language: En un V.I.P., un V.I.P., ey
sentences.grammarNote:
**en un V.I.P.** = in a VIP (area/section) — **en** for location.
**ey** = a common exclamation/filler in reggaetón, from English 'hey'. Many reggaetón songs blend Spanish with English expressions.

**Dile** = tell her/say to her — imperative of **decir** with the indirect object pronoun **le** fused to it.
The repetition of this line is a refrain (estribillo) — a common song structure in reggaetón.

**Vámonos** = let's go — the reflexive form of **vamos**, more emphatic.
**pa'l** = **para el** — double contraction in colloquial speech: **para** → **pa'** + **el** → **pa'l**.
This kind of elision is extremely common in fast Caribbean Spanish.
